Feel free to navigate to your desired cheat sheets below:

MySQL is an open-source Relational Database Management System (RDBMS) that organizes data into rows and columns. It uses Structured Query Language (SQL) that allows users to retrieve, store, and manage information.
Feel free to navigate to your desired cheat sheets below: note This is a work in progress...
MySQL

MySQL is an open-source Relational Database Management System (RDBMS) that organizes data into rows and columns. It uses Structured Query Language (SQL) that allows users to retrieve, store, and manage information.
These are a category of SQL commands used to modify the structure (schema) of a database.

A database is a structured collection of interrelated data stored to serve multiple applications. Syntax​ CREATE DATABASE [IF NOT EXISTS] database_name; Example​ CREATE DATABASE IF NOT EXISTS university_db;

A table is a structured collection of related data organized into rows and columns. Syntax​ CREATE TABLE [IF NOT EXISTS] table_name (column_1 datatype_1 [key_1] [constraint_1], ...) [COMMENT = 'desired_comment']; Example​ CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS students ( student_id INT PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT, first_name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL, last_name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL, email VARCHAR(100) UNIQUE, enrollment_date DATE DEFAULT (CURRENT_DATE), gpa DECIMAL(3,2) CHECK (gpa >= 0 AND gpa <= 4.0) ) COMMENT = 'Stores student records for university';

An index is a specialized data structure that improves the speed of data retrieval operations on a table. Syntax​ CREATE [UNIQUE | FULLTEXT | SPATIAL] INDEX index_name ON table_name (column_list); Example​ Non-unique index. CREATE INDEX idx_student_lastname ON students(last_name); Unique composite index. CREATE UNIQUE INDEX idx_unique_name ON students(first_name, last_name);

A view is a virtual table that does not store data physically (except in materialized views) but dynamically fetches results based on a stored query. Syntax​ CREATE [OR REPLACE] VIEW view_name AS SELECT_statement; Example​ CREATE VIEW active_students AS SELECT student_id, first_name, last_name, gpa FROM students WHERE enrollment_date >= '2023-01-01';

Change the name of an existing table. Syntax​ ALTER TABLE table_name RENAME TO desired_name; Example​ ALTER TABLE students RENAME TO learners; Add a new column to an existing table. Syntax​ ALTER TABLE table_name ADD COLUMN column_name datatype [AFTER desired_column]; Example​ ALTER TABLE students ADD COLUMN phone VARCHAR(20) AFTER email; Add a new constraint to an existing column. Syntax​ ALTER TABLE table_name ADD CONSTRAINT constraint_name CHECK (column_name <condition>); Example​ ALTER TABLE students ADD CONSTRAINT chk_gpa CHECK (gpa >= 0 AND gpa <= 4.0); Change the name of an existing column. Syntax​ ALTER TABLE table_name RENAME COLUMN column_name TO desired_name; Example​ ALTER TABLE students RENAME COLUMN gpa TO grade_point_average; Change the definition of an existing column. Syntax​ ALTER TABLE table_name MODIFY COLUMN column_name datatype; Example​ ALTER TABLE students MODIFY COLUMN phone VARCHAR(15); Remove an existing constraint in an existing column. Syntax​ ALTER TABLE table_name DROP CHECK constraint_name; Example​ ALTER TABLE students DROP CHECK chk_gpa; Remove an existing column. Syntax​ ALTER TABLE table_name DROP COLUMN column_name; Example​ ALTER TABLE students DROP COLUMN phone; Add an index to an existing column. Syntax​ ALTER TABLE table_name ADD INDEX index_name (column_name); Example​ ALTER TABLE students ADD INDEX idx_email (email); Remove an existing index. Syntax​ ALTER TABLE table_name DROP INDEX index_name; Example​ ALTER TABLE students DROP INDEX idx_email;
Change the query definition of a view. Syntax​ ALTER VIEW view_name AS SELECT_statement; Example​ ALTER VIEW active_students AS SELECT student_id, first_name, last_name FROM students WHERE gpa > 3.0;
Remove an existing database and all of its contents where action cannot be rolled back. Syntax​ DROP DATABASE database_name; Example​ DROP DATABASE university_db;
Remove an existing table definition along with all its data, constraints, and indexes (Cannot be rolled back). Syntax​ DROP TABLE table_name; Example​ DROP TABLE students;
Remove an existing index. Syntax​ DROP INDEX index_name ON table_name; Example​ DROP INDEX idx_student_lastname ON students;
Remove an existing view. Syntax​ DROP VIEW view_name; Example​ DROP VIEW active_students;

Fast-remove all rows from a table by dropping and re-creating the table structure, resetting auto-increment counters (Cannot be rolled back). Syntax​ TRUNCATE TABLE table_name; Example​ TRUNCATE TABLE students;
Change the name of an existing table. Syntax​ RENAME TABLE table_name TO desired_name; Example​ RENAME TABLE students TO university_students;
These are a category of SQL commands used to modify the data of a table in a database.

Insert a single row into a table by optionally specifying column names or omit to insert all in order. Syntax​ INSERT INTO table_name [(column1, column2, ...)] VALUES (value1, value2, ...); Example​ INSERT INTO students (first_name, last_name, email, gpa) VALUES ('Michael', 'Jackson', 'michael.jackson@university.edu', 3.8); Insert multiple rows into a table by optionally writing more than one INSERT statement or a single statement for efficiency. Syntax​ INSERT INTO table_name [(column1, column2, ...)] VALUES (row1_value1, row1_value2, ...), (row2_value1, row2_value2, ...), ...; Example​ INSERT INTO students (first_name, last_name, gpa) VALUES ('Henry', 'Cavill', 3.9), ('Gun', 'Park', 3.4), ('Vineeth', 'Chivukula', 3.7); Insert a single row by explicitly assigning values directly to columns. Syntax​ INSERT INTO table_name SET column1 = value1, column2 = value2, ...; Example​ INSERT INTO students SET first_name = 'Clark', last_name = 'Kent', gpa = 4.0; Copy data from another table or query result. Syntax​ INSERT INTO table_name [(column1, column2, ...)] SELECT_statement; Example​ INSERT INTO students_archive (student_id, first_name, last_name) SELECT student_id, first_name, last_name FROM students WHERE enrollment_date < '2022-01-01'; Insert data into a table using default values. Syntax​ INSERT INTO table_name (column1, column2, column3, ...) VALUES (row1_value1, DEFAULT, row1_value3, ...); Example​ INSERT INTO students (first_name, last_name, enrollment_date) VALUES ('Jensen', 'Ackles', DEFAULT);

Update existing rows in a table. Syntax​ UPDATE table_name SET column1 = value1, column2 = value2, ... WHERE condition; Example​ UPDATE students SET gpa = 3.85, last_name = 'Jones' WHERE student_id = 101; Update existing rows in a table using an expression. Syntax​ UPDATE table_name SET column1 = expression1, column2 = expression2, ... WHERE condition; Example​ UPDATE students SET gpa = gpa + 0.1 WHERE gpa < 3.5;
Delete existing rows in a table by optionally specifying the condition. Syntax​ DELETE FROM table_name [WHERE condition]; Example​ DELETE FROM students WHERE student_id = 999;

Replace a single row in a table. Syntax​ REPLACE INTO table_name (column1, column2, column3, ...) VALUES (value1, value2, ...); Example​ REPLACE INTO students (student_id, first_name, last_name, gpa) VALUES (101, 'John', 'Wick', 3.9); Replace multiple rows in a table. Syntax​ REPLACE INTO table_name (column1, column2, column3, ...) SELECT_statement; Example​ REPLACE INTO students_archive (student_id, first_name, last_name) SELECT student_id, first_name, last_name FROM students WHERE gpa > 3.8;
These are a category of SQL commands used to retrieve data from a table.

It is the absolute foundational read-only command used to retrieve data from tables. Syntax​ SELECT [ALL | DISTINCT] select_list FROM table_name [AS alias] [JOIN clauses] [WHERE condition] [GROUP BY group_columns] [HAVING group_condition] [ORDER BY order_columns [ASC|DESC]] [LIMIT [offset,] row_count]; Examples​ Select all columns and rows. SELECT * FROM students; Select specific columns. SELECT first_name, last_name FROM students; Select specific columns with a condition. SELECT first_name, last_name, gpa FROM students WHERE gpa > 3.5; -- Can be <, =, >=, <= Select unique values of a column. SELECT DISTINCT department FROM courses; Alias specific columns while selecting. SELECT first_name AS 'First Name', gpa AS 'Grade Average' FROM students; Select all columns where their values lie within a range. SELECT * FROM students WHERE enrollment_date BETWEEN '2023-01-01' AND '2023-12-31'; Select all columns where their values match a specific pattern. SELECT * FROM students WHERE last_name LIKE 'sm%'; -- Can be %sm (e.g, Lookism), %sm% (e.g, Jasmine) Select all columns where their values match in a defined list. SELECT * FROM students WHERE student_id IN (101, 105, 110); Select all columns where their values satisfy all the mentioned conditions. SELECT * FROM students WHERE gpa > 3.0 AND enrollment_date > '2024-01-01'; Select all columns where their values satisfy either of the mentioned conditions. SELECT * FROM students WHERE department = 'CS' OR department = 'Math'; Select all columns where their values are not NULL. SELECT * FROM students WHERE email IS NOT NULL; Return rows where matching values exist in both tables. SELECT s.first_name, s.last_name, m.major_name FROM students s INNER JOIN majors m ON s.major_id = m.major_id; Return all rows from the left table and matching rows from the right table (show NULL for unmatched rows). SELECT s.first_name, s.last_name, e.course_id FROM students s LEFT JOIN enrollments e ON s.student_id = e.student_id; Return all rows from the right table and matching rows from the left table (show NULL for unmatched rows). SELECT c.course_name, e.student_id FROM enrollments e RIGHT JOIN courses c ON e.course_id = c.course_id; Return every row from the first table paired with every row from the second. SELECT s.first_name, c.course_name FROM students s CROSS JOIN courses c; Return rows from a table by matching them against other rows within the same table, treating it as two separate entities using aliases. SELECT e1.first_name AS employee_name, e2.first_name AS manager_name FROM employees e1 LEFT JOIN employees e2 ON e1.manager_id = e2.employee_id; Group rows with identical values in the specified column where all the non-aggregated columns in SELECT must be in GROUP BY. SELECT department, COUNT(*) AS student_count, AVG(gpa) AS avg_gpa FROM students GROUP BY department; Group rows with identical values in the specified columns where all the non-aggregated columns in SELECT must be in GROUP BY. SELECT department, enrollment_year, COUNT(*) AS cohort_size FROM students GROUP BY department, enrollment_year; Filter groups based on some condition. SELECT department, COUNT(*) AS student_count, AVG(gpa) AS avg_gpa FROM students WHERE enrollment_date >= '2023-01-01' GROUP BY department HAVING AVG(gpa) > 3.2 AND COUNT(*) >= 5; Sort the results in ascending order based on a column. SELECT * FROM students ORDER BY first_name; -- Default is ASC Sort the results in descending order based on a column. SELECT * FROM students ORDER BY gpa DESC; Sort the results in the desired order based on multiple columns. SELECT * FROM students ORDER BY first_name ASC, gpa DESC; Restrict the number of rows. SELECT * FROM students LIMIT 7; Restrict the number of rows after skipping a specific offset. SELECT * FROM students LIMIT 7 OFFSET 6; -- Can also be written as SELECT * FROM students LIMIT 6, 7; Filter rows based on a single dynamically calculated value from an inner query. SELECT first_name, last_name, gpa FROM students WHERE gpa > (SELECT AVG(gpa) FROM students); -- Known as a subquery Filter rows by matching against a dynamically generated list of multiple values. SELECT first_name, last_name FROM students WHERE student_id IN (SELECT student_id FROM enrollments WHERE status = 'active'); Treat the results of an inner query as a temporary, dynamically filtered table. SELECT MAX(early_students.gpa) AS highest_gpa FROM (SELECT gpa FROM students WHERE enrollment_date < '2026-01-01') AS early_students; Append a dynamically calculated scalar value as a brand-new column next to every row. SELECT first_name, last_name, gpa, (SELECT AVG(gpa) FROM students) AS school_average FROM students; Combine the result sets of two queries, automatically removing duplicate rows. SELECT student_id, first_name, last_name FROM students UNION SELECT student_id, first_name, last_name FROM students_archive; Combine the result sets of two queries, preserving all rows including duplicates. SELECT student_id, first_name, last_name FROM students UNION ALL SELECT student_id, first_name, last_name FROM students_archive; Return only the distinct rows that are present in the result sets of both queries. SELECT student_id, first_name, last_name FROM students INTERSECT SELECT student_id, first_name, last_name FROM students_archive; Return distinct rows from the first query that do not exist in the result set of the second query. SELECT student_id, first_name, last_name FROM students EXCEPT SELECT student_id, first_name, last_name FROM students_archive; Evaluate a list of sequential conditions and return a specific value for the first true condition encountered (Searched CASE). SELECT first_name, gpa, CASE WHEN gpa >= 3.5 THEN 'Honors List' WHEN gpa >= 2.0 THEN 'Good Standing' ELSE 'Academic Probation' END AS academic_status FROM students;

The chronological sequence in which the database engine processes each clause and returns data: FROM | JOIN -> WHERE -> GROUP BY -> HAVING -> SELECT -> DISTINCT -> ORDER BY -> LIMIT | OFFSET Example​ SELECT c.department AS dept_name, AVG(s.gpa) AS average_gpa -- (5) Evaluates columns & aliases FROM students s -- (1) Finds the primary data source INNER JOIN enrollments e ON s.student_id = e.student_id -- (1) Merges enrollment data INNER JOIN courses c ON e.course_id = c.course_id -- (1) Merges course details WHERE e.status = 'active' AND c.course_level > 100 -- (2) Filters base rows before grouping GROUP BY c.department -- (3) Groups remaining rows into buckets HAVING AVG(s.gpa) >= 3.0 -- (4) Filters aggregated group buckets ORDER BY average_gpa DESC -- (6) Sorts the final output using the alias LIMIT 2; -- (7) Restricts total rows returned

These are functions that perform calculations on multiple rows of a single column to return a single summarized value. Syntax​ SELECT AGGREGATE_FUNCTION([DISTINCT] column_name | *) FROM table_name; Examples​ Count the total number of records in a table including rows with NULL values. SELECT COUNT(*) FROM students; Count the total number of non-null values in a specific column. SELECT COUNT(email) FROM students; Count the total number of unique values in a column by eliminating duplicates. SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT department) FROM courses; Calculate the total arithmetic sum of all numeric values in a column. SELECT SUM(tuition_paid) FROM invoices; Calculate the mathematical mean of all numeric values in a column, automatically ignoring NULL values. SELECT AVG(gpa) FROM students; Find the highest numeric value, latest date, or last alphabetical string in a column. SELECT MAX(gpa) FROM students; Find the lowest numeric value, earliest date, or first alphabetical string in a column. SELECT MIN(enrollment_date) FROM students;

These are scalar functions that perform operations on text data to manipulate, format, or extract characters from strings. Syntax​ SELECT STRING_FUNCTION(string_column | 'text_literal' [, arguments]) FROM table_name; Examples​ Convert all characters in a string to uppercase. SELECT UPPER(first_name) FROM students; Convert all characters in a string to lowercase. SELECT LOWER(first_name) FROM students; Calculate the total number of characters in a string, including spaces and punctuation in bytes. SELECT LENGTH(last_name) FROM students; Return the character length of a string specifically counting multibyte characters as a single character. SELECT CHAR_LENGTH(last_name) FROM students; Combine two or more strings together into a single text output. SELECT CONCAT(first_name, ' ', last_name) AS full_name FROM students; Combine two or more strings together with a separator into a single text output. SELECT CONCAT_WS(', ', first_name, last_name) AS full_name_ws FROM students; Extract a specific portion of a text string based on a starting position and a defined length. SELECT SUBSTRING(email, 1, 5) FROM students; -- Extracts the first 5 characters Replace all occurrences of a specified substring within a text string with a new text value. SELECT REPLACE(email, 'university.edu', 'alumni.edu') FROM students; Remove any leading and trailing space characters from a text string. SELECT TRIM(' Patrick Bateman ') AS cleaned_name FROM students; Remove any leading space characters from a text string. SELECT LTRIM(' Ned Luke') AS cleaned_name FROM students; Remove any trailing space characters from a text string. SELECT RTRIM('Ada Wong ') AS cleaned_name FROM students; Find the first occurrence position of a substring inside a target string (returns 0 if not found). SELECT INSTR(email, '@') AS at_symbol_position FROM students; Pad the left side of a string with a specific character string until it reaches a desired total length. SELECT LPAD(student_id, 8, '0') AS padded_id FROM students; -- Turns '123' into '00000123' Pad the right side of a string with a specific character string until it reaches a desired total length. SELECT RPAD(department, 5, '.') AS formatted_dept FROM courses; -- Turns 'CS' into 'CS...' Reverse the order of all characters within a string. SELECT REVERSE(first_name) FROM students;

These are scalar functions used to retrieve, manipulate, format, or perform calculations on date, time, and timestamp values. Syntax​ SELECT DATE_FUNCTION([date_column | 'date_literal' [, arguments]]) FROM table_name; Examples​ Return the current system date and time as a timestamp value. SELECT NOW() AS current_timestamp_now; -- Format: YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS Return the current system date value without the time component. SELECT CURDATE() AS current_date_today; -- Format: YYYY-MM-DD Extract the four-digit year from a date value. SELECT YEAR(enrollment_date) FROM students; Extract the month number (1 to 12) from a date value. SELECT MONTH(enrollment_date) FROM students; Extract the day of the month (1 to 31) from a date value. SELECT DAY(enrollment_date) FROM students; Add a specified time interval value to a date or datetime value. SELECT DATE_ADD(enrollment_date, INTERVAL 4 YEAR) AS graduation_date FROM students; Subtract a specified time interval value from a date or datetime value. SELECT DATE_SUB(enrollment_date, INTERVAL 6 MONTH) AS orientation_date FROM students; Calculate the total number of days between two distinct date values (expressed as Date1 - Date2). SELECT DATEDIFF('2026-06-06', enrollment_date) AS days_enrolled FROM students; Format a date value into a customized string format based on specified format specifier codes. SELECT DATE_FORMAT(NOW(), '%Y-%m-%d %H:%i:%s'); Extract only the time component from a combined datetime or timestamp expression. SELECT TIME(NOW()) AS current_time_only; -- Format: HH:MM:SS

These are scalar functions used to perform mathematical calculations, rounding operations, or algebraic manipulations on numeric data. Syntax​ SELECT NUMERIC_FUNCTION(numeric_column | number_literal [, arguments]) FROM table_name; Examples​ Return the absolute, positive value of a number by stripping its negative sign. SELECT ABS(-3.5) AS absolute_value FROM students; -- Returns 3.5 Round a numeric value to a specified number of decimal places. SELECT ROUND(gpa, 1) FROM students; -- e.g., 3.85 becomes 3.9 Truncate a numeric value down to a specified number of decimal places without rounding. SELECT TRUNCATE(gpa, 1) FROM students; -- e.g., 3.85 becomes 3.8 Round a numeric value up to the next nearest whole integer. SELECT CEIL(gpa) FROM students; -- e.g., 3.1 becomes 4.0 Round a numeric value down to the previous nearest whole integer. SELECT FLOOR(gpa) FROM students; -- e.g., 3.9 becomes 3.0 Calculate the remainder of a division operation between two numbers. SELECT MOD(student_id, 2) AS is_odd FROM students; -- Returns 0 for even IDs, 1 for odd IDs Raise a base number to the exponential power of a specified exponent value. SELECT POW(gpa, 2) FROM students; -- Calculates gpa squared Calculate the non-negative square root of a numeric value. SELECT SQRT(9) AS square_root FROM students; -- Returns 3.0 Return the mathematical constant Pi (π). SELECT PI() AS pi_value FROM students; -- Returns 3.141593

These are conditional scalar functions used to handle, replace, or evaluate missing or unassigned values (NULL) within your dataset. Syntax​ SELECT NULL_FUNCTION(expression1 [, expression2, ...]) FROM table_name; Examples​ Return the second argument if the first argument evaluates to a NULL value. SELECT IFNULL(email, 'no-email@university.edu') AS contact_email FROM students; Return the very first non-null value from a specified list of arguments evaluated from left to right. SELECT COALESCE(phone, email, 'No Contact Info Available') AS primary_contact FROM students; Compare two arguments and return NULL if they are equal; otherwise, return the first argument. SELECT NULLIF(department, 'Undeclared') AS verified_dept FROM students; -- Returns NULL if department is 'Undeclared' Evaluate a condition and return a specified value if it is true, and an alternative value if it is false. SELECT IF(gpa >= 2.0, 'Good Standing', 'Academic Probation') AS student_status FROM students;

These are advanced functions that perform calculations across a specific set of table rows (a "window") that are relationally related to the current row, preserving the individual identity of each row in the final output. Syntax​ SELECT WINDOW_FUNCTION(expression) OVER ( [PARTITION BY partition_columns] [ORDER BY sort_columns] [ROWS|RANGE frame_spec] ) FROM table_name; Examples​ Assign a unique, sequential integer to each row starting at 1 within every department partition. SELECT department, last_name, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY department ORDER BY gpa DESC) AS rank_in_dept FROM students; Assign a rank to each row within a partition where tied values receive the same rank, leaving gaps in the sequence afterward. SELECT first_name, gpa, RANK() OVER (ORDER BY gpa DESC) AS national_rank FROM students; -- Tied GPAs get the same rank (e.g., 1, 2, 2, 4) Assign a rank to each row within a partition where tied values receive the same rank, preserving a continuous numerical sequence without gaps. SELECT first_name, gpa, DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY gpa DESC) AS continuous_rank FROM students; -- Tied GPAs get the same rank with no gaps (e.g., 1, 2, 2, 3) Calculate a running or partition-wide aggregate total without compressing individual table rows. SELECT department, first_name, tuition_paid, SUM(tuition_paid) OVER (PARTITION BY department) AS total_dept_revenue FROM students; Calculate a cumulative running total from the start of the partition up to the current row. SELECT department, enrollment_date, tuition_paid, SUM(tuition_paid) OVER ( PARTITION BY department ORDER BY enrollment_date ASC ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW ) AS rolling_total FROM students; Retrieve a value from a previous row within the same partition based on a designated physical offset. SELECT student_id, enrollment_date, LAG(enrollment_date, 1) OVER (ORDER BY enrollment_date ASC) AS previous_student_date FROM students; Calculate a rolling average using a physical frame of the current row and the immediate two rows preceding it. SELECT enrollment_date, gpa, AVG(gpa) OVER ( ORDER BY enrollment_date ASC ROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW ) AS moving_avg_3_students FROM students; Retrieve a value from a succeeding row within the same partition based on a designated physical offset. SELECT student_id, enrollment_date, LEAD(enrollment_date, 1) OVER (ORDER BY enrollment_date ASC) AS next_student_date FROM students; Calculate a moving aggregate using values based on the data value itself (logical range) rather than physical row positions. SELECT student_id, gpa, COUNT(*) OVER ( ORDER BY gpa RANGE BETWEEN 0.1 PRECEDING AND 0.1 FOLLOWING ) AS peers_within_point_one_gpa FROM students; Divide the ordered rows within a partition into a specified number of roughly equal, ranked groups (tiles). SELECT first_name, gpa, NTILE(4) OVER (ORDER BY gpa DESC) AS gpa_quartile FROM students; -- Divides the student body into 4 performance quartiles (1 to 4)

A Common Table Expression (CTE) is a temporary named result set that you can reference within a subsequent SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE statement. They act as virtual tables that exist only for the duration of that single query execution, making complex queries far more readable than deeply nested subqueries. Syntax​ WITH cte_name [(column_list)] AS ( SELECT_statement_defining_the_cte ) SELECT_statement_using_the_cte; Examples​ Define a single temporary result set to pre-calculate high-performing students, then filter the virtual dataset in the main query. WITH honor_roll AS ( SELECT student_id, first_name, last_name, gpa FROM students WHERE gpa >= 3.5 ) SELECT first_name, last_name, gpa FROM honor_roll WHERE gpa > 3.9; Define multiple, comma-separated CTEs where the second CTE references the first one to create a modular data processing pipeline. WITH dept_averages AS ( SELECT department, AVG(gpa) AS avg_gpa FROM students GROUP BY department ), top_departments AS ( SELECT department, avg_gpa FROM dept_averages WHERE avg_gpa >= 3.0 ) SELECT d.department, d.avg_gpa, c.title FROM top_departments d INNER JOIN courses c ON d.department = c.department;

A specialized variation of a CTE that references itself. It is used to traverse hierarchical, graph-based, or tree-structured data by looping through rows until a termination condition is met. Syntax​ WITH RECURSIVE cte_name AS ( -- Anchor Member: Establishes the base starting row(s) SELECT_statement1 UNION [ALL] -- Recursive Member: References cte_name to loop through next levels SELECT_statement2 WHERE termination_condition ) SELECT * FROM cte_name; Examples​ Traverse a hierarchical course prerequisite chain to find all sequential courses required to unlock an advanced 400-level course. WITH RECURSIVE course_path AS ( -- Anchor Member: Start with the advanced target course SELECT course_id, title, prerequisite_id FROM courses WHERE title = 'Advanced Quantum Physics' UNION ALL -- Recursive Member: Join the CTE back to the base table to find the next prerequisite row SELECT c.course_id, c.title, c.prerequisite_id FROM courses c INNER JOIN course_path cp ON c.course_id = cp.prerequisite_id ) SELECT * FROM course_path;
